component_identification.content: Decrypto contains 110 keyword cards (440 total words), 48 code cards, 4 screen cards (2 per team), 2 notepads, and rulebook. Each team uses one screen to hide their four keyword slots and maintains a public guess/history sheet. Interception and miscommunication markers are tracked on paper in standard play.

core_game_loop.content: Turn rhythm: active side reveals or frames information, teammates/opponents respond under that game’s communication limits, then resolve points or survival state and rotate responsibility. Teach this as a repeating three-step cycle: present signal, interpret signal, then resolve consequences. New players should always know whose decision matters right now and what hidden information cannot be shared. Most confusion comes from breaking communication restrictions or skipping reveal order, so keep sequence strict.

detailed_rules.content: Form two teams behind screens. Each team draws 4 keyword cards and places one in each numbered slot (1–4) visible only to team. Each round, one clue-giver draws a 3-digit code card (e.g., 3-1-4) and gives three clues in order, each clue mapping to the team keyword in that position. Teammates guess the code sequence; opposing team listens and logs clue history to infer mappings over time. If your own team guesses wrong, gain one Miscommunication mark. If opponents guess exactly, they gain one Interception mark. First team with 2 Interceptions wins; if a team gets 2 Miscommunications they lose immediately. Restrictions: clues cannot be translations, direct word fragments, or obvious derivatives of keyword; once clues spoken, no editing. Strong play balances teammate clarity and opponent ambiguity.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Primary win by earning 2 Interception tokens (correctly reading enemy code twice). Automatic loss if your team accumulates 2 Miscommunication tokens. If both conditions would occur in same round, resolve using rulebook sequence (interception/miscommunication order) and then check winner.

beginner_strategy.content: 1) Clues should be internally consistent across rounds so teammates build mapping memory. 2) Avoid one-to-one obvious synonyms that hand interceptions to opponents. 3) Opponent logs are powerful—maintain your own deduction sheet carefully. 4) When ahead, choose safer clue clarity to avoid miscommunication losses. 5) When behind, accept slightly riskier abstraction to deny second interception.

metadata.notes: Check 4 failed: total_token_count is 440; must be 800-3000 (hard reject if <800 or >5000)
